---
title: "Sun Protection"
description: |
  Crispy is not always better
date: '2026-07-04'
image: "dontfryday.png"
categories:
    - Adventure
format: html
toc: true
lightbox: true
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  error: false
  message: false
draft: false
---

```{r}
library(here)
source(here("packages.R"))
source(here("functions.R"))

series_filter = 17

data = pollresults(series_filter)
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-overview
#| fig-cap: "Votes and shares for each question (faceted)."
#| fig-width: 9
#| fig-height: 10.5
faceted_bar_plot(data)
```

# How decisive were these results?

Hat is the odd one out this week with three options, so it gets its own test; everything else is a straight two-way race.

```{r}
#| label: summary-stats
summary_sp = summarize_two_option(data)
lopsided_sp = summary_sp %>% arrange(p_value_50_50)
closest_sp  = summary_sp %>% arrange(desc(p_value_50_50)) %>% slice(1)
```

**TL;DR:** `r lopsided_sp$question[1]` was the most one-sided call of the week — `r lopsided_sp$leader[1]` beat `r lopsided_sp$trailer[1]` `r lopsided_sp$leader_votes[1]`–`r lopsided_sp$trailer_votes[1]` (`r percent(lopsided_sp$leader_share[1],1)`), with Sunscreen right behind it. `r closest_sp$question[1]` was the real toss-up: `r closest_sp$leader[1]` edged `r closest_sp$trailer[1]` `r closest_sp$leader_votes[1]`–`r closest_sp$trailer_votes[1]`, about as close to a coin flip as it gets.

```{r}
#| label: summary-table
binom_summary_table(summary_sp)
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-leader-ci
#| fig-cap: "Leader shares with 95% confidence intervals (two-option questions only)."
#| fig-width: 7
#| fig-height: 3.5
leader_ci_plot(summary_sp)
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-waffle-sunscreen
#| fig-cap: "One square per vote: Sunscreen."
#| fig-width: 6
#| fig-height: 4
waffle_question(data, "Sunscreen")
```

## The three-way race: Hat

```{r}
#| label: hat-chisq
hat = data %>% filter(question == "Hat") %>% arrange(desc(votes))
hat_chisq = chisq.test(hat$votes)
```

```{r}
#| label: hat-table
hat %>%
  transmute(option, votes, share = percent(share, 0.1)) %>%
  kableExtra::kable()
```

Baseball Cap isn't just the leader, it's the clear favorite: `r percent(hat$share[1], 1)` of the vote. `r hat$option[2]` (`r percent(hat$share[2], 1)`) pulls ahead of `r hat$option[3]` (`r percent(hat$share[3], 1)`) for a distant second, no longer the even split it looked like before RTP weighed in. A chi-square goodness-of-fit test against "all three hats equally popular" gives p = `r signif(hat_chisq$p.value, 3)` — nowhere close to what we'd expect if hat preference were random. Both offices have a type, too: HQ and RTP each independently put Baseball Cap on top.

# Does HQ agree with RTP?

RTP's votes are in for this poll, and it's the most eventful HQ/RTP comparison we've had yet.

```{r}
#| label: hq-rtp-stats
hq_rtp_sp = hq_rtp_compare(data)
alignment_sp = cor(hq_rtp_sp$hq_share, hq_rtp_sp$rtp_share)
cumulative_sp = cumulative_alignment(17)
flip_count_sp = flip_count_so_far(17)
most_significant_sp = hq_rtp_sp %>% arrange(p_value) %>% slice(1)

flips_sp = data %>%
  group_by(question) %>%
  filter(n() == 2, sum(rtp_votes) > 0) %>%
  group_modify(~ {
    hq_winner  = .x$option[which.max(.x$hq_votes)]
    rtp_winner = .x$option[which.max(.x$rtp_votes)]
    tibble(hq_winner, rtp_winner, flipped = hq_winner != rtp_winner)
  }) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  filter(flipped)
```

```{r}
#| label: hq-rtp-table
hq_rtp_sp %>%
  transmute(
    question,
    `Leader (overall)` = option,
    `HQ share`  = sprintf("%s (%d/%d)", percent(hq_share, 1), hq_votes, hq_N),
    `RTP share` = sprintf("%s (%d/%d)", percent(rtp_share, 1), rtp_votes, rtp_N),
    `Gap (pts)` = percent(abs(gap), 1),
    `p-value`   = signif(p_value, 3)
  ) %>%
  kableExtra::kable()
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-hq-rtp
#| fig-cap: "HQ vs RTP support for each question's overall winner."
#| fig-width: 7
#| fig-height: 4
hq_rtp_plot(hq_rtp_sp)
```

Two milestones this week. First, `r most_significant_sp$question[1]` clears p < 0.05 for the first time since RTP joined the poll — HQ went `r percent(most_significant_sp$hq_share[1], 1)` for `r most_significant_sp$option[1]`, RTP only `r percent(most_significant_sp$rtp_share[1], 1)` (p = `r signif(most_significant_sp$p_value[1], 2)`). Every office gap before this one had a p-value comfortably above 0.1 — this is the first one we can call a real difference rather than noise.

Second, the Alignment Index drops to **`r round(alignment_sp, 2)`** this week, its lowest reading yet (cumulative across the whole RTP era: **`r round(cumulative_sp, 2)`**, so the long-run picture is still solidly aligned — one rough week doesn't undo five good ones). And `r flips_sp$question[1]` flipped outright: HQ's real preference was `r flips_sp$hq_winner[1]`, RTP's was `r flips_sp$rtp_winner[1]`, and the combined total ended up siding with RTP. That brings the running tally to **`r flip_count_sp` questions** since RTP joined where the two offices would have crowned different winners on their own.

# Do the sun-safety-conscious cluster together?

Reapplying sunscreen and reaching for aloe after a burn both sound like the habits of the same sun-safety-minded person. Let's bound the overlap.

```{r}
#| label: overlap-method-burncare
ov_safe = overlap_bounds(
  a_total = data %>% filter(question == "Method", option == "Reapply Sunscreen") %>% pull(votes),
  N_a     = data %>% filter(question == "Method") %>% pull(N) %>% unique(),
  b_total = data %>% filter(question == "Burn Care", option == "Aloe") %>% pull(votes),
  N_b     = data %>% filter(question == "Burn Care") %>% pull(N) %>% unique()
)
```

Of the `r ov_safe$m` overlapping voters, somewhere between `r ov_safe$min_overlap` and `r ov_safe$max_overlap` (`r percent(ov_safe$min_overlap/ov_safe$m, 1)`–`r percent(ov_safe$max_overlap/ov_safe$m, 1)`) could be both Reapply-Sunscreen *and* Aloe people, versus about `r round(ov_safe$expected_overlap)` (`r percent(ov_safe$expected_overlap/ov_safe$m, 1)`) expected if the two habits were unrelated. Given how popular both options already are on their own, there's a good floor under the "actually sun-safe" coalition — good news for everyone's skin.
